paper.title: When Does Combining Language Models Help? A Co-Failure Ceiling on Routing, Voting, and Mixture-of-Agents Across 67 Frontier Models

claims.statement: Any selection policy whose output is a member model's answer (router, vote, cascade) has accuracy at most 1 − β, where β = Pr[all models wrong].

evidence.description: Proof: On the all-wrong event, every member answer differs from the label; a selection policy outputs one member answer, hence is wrong. Therefore Acc(π) ≤ Pr[not all wrong] = 1 − β, attained by the per-query oracle.

evidence.location: Paper §5, Table 2, Table 3; MATH-500 β=0.052, code β=0.079, free-response GPQA β=0.127
evidence.description: Empirical β measurements across domains confirm the ceiling is populated on open-ended tasks, with accuracy upper bounds of 1−β = 0.948 (MATH-500 oracle) through 0.921 (code contests oracle).

claims.statement: A Clopper-Pearson lower confidence bound on β turns one held-out query set into a $0 pre-deployment certificate on the largest gain any selection policy can deliver over single-best.

evidence.description: K~Binomial(n,β) since queries are i.i.d. The Clopper-Pearson lower limit obeys Pr[β ≥ β_lo] ≥ 1−δ. With probability ≥ 1−δ, (1−β) ≤ (1−β_lo) and Acc−a_sb ≤ (1−β_lo)−a_sb. A union bound replaces a_sb by its upper confidence bound.
Standalone tool that from K/n and a_sb returns the certified $0 lower bound on maximum gain.

claims.statement: Pairwise error correlation ρ cannot identify the co-failure rate β for m ≥ 3 models; distributions with identical 1D and 2D marginals (hence identical marginal error rates and pairwise correlations) can have different β.

evidence.description: Take m=3, p₁=½, p₂=¼. Both (q₀,q₁,q₂,q₃)=(¼,0,¾,0) and (0,¾,0,¼) reproduce every pairwise marginal yet have β=0 and β=¼ respectively. Padding with independent coordinates extends to any m≥3.
Common-shock mixture (β_∞=0.05) vs. matched-ρ̄ Gaussian copula (β_∞=0): ratio past 10⁷× at large m despite identical pairwise statistics at m=2.

claims.statement: A correctly tetrachoric-calibrated single-factor Gaussian copula under the pairwise error correlation systematically underprices the empirical co-failure tail on open-ended tasks, with the bias growing in pool size.

evidence.description: Common-shock model: β(m)=π+(1−π)α₀^m → π>0 while β_sf(m) → 0 (Gaussian copula with ρ̄<1 has zero lower tail dependence). Ratio →∞ and strictly increasing in m, equalling 1 at m=2.
MATH-500 (67 models): β=0.052 vs. β_sf=0.021, ratio 2.5× (90% CI 1.7-3.4). Replicated on code: 3.1× (CI [1.5,6.2] excluding 1). Pool-size effect: ratio rises from 1.0 at k=2 to median 2.5 at k=67.

claims.statement: Even the full 67×67 Gaussian copula, Monte-Carlo-integrated over all pairwise-tetrachoric correlations, underprices the empirical co-failure tail; the residual is 2.25× on MATH-500.

evidence.description: Full 67×67 pairwise-tetrachoric matrix (projected to nearest PSD: 26 negative eigenvalues, mean ρ drops 0.78→0.74), 4×10⁵ MC draws: β_full-Σ=0.023 vs. empirical 0.052 = 2.25× excess. Opposite direction of PSD artifact (which would inflate, not reduce, ratio).
Exchangeable Clayton copula (λ_L=0.69) calibrated to same mean co-failure: β=0.026 vs. 0.052 (1.96×). Not a Gaussian tail-dependence artifact.

claims.statement: The driver is a common-mode atom (positive-probability joint failure event), not smooth tail dependence as measured by λ_L.

evidence.description: Clayton copula (λ_L=0.71): 4.0× at m=53, smaller (2.6×) for larger λ_L=0.84 because higher λ_L raises ρ that single-factor absorbs. Common-shock with rare shared-failure atom (β_∞=0.05): ratio past 10⁷×. Distinction: pairwise ρ can absorb smooth tail dependence but not common-mode atom.

claims.statement: Two regimes exist: ceiling-bound (open-ended math and code, where β>0 caps all policies) and realizability-bound (multiple-choice science, where β≈0 and the oracle gain is resolvable disagreement). Pairwise ρ cannot tell them apart.

evidence.description: MATH-500: β=0.052, ceiling-bound. Code: β=0.079, ceiling-bound. Multiple-choice GPQA: β≈0 (0/130), realizability-bound, G=0.154 resolvable. Free-response GPQA (same content): β=0.127, ceiling-bound. ρ values: 0.53, 0.27, 0.25 — overlap between regimes.

claims.statement: Answer format (open-ended vs. multiple-choice), not subject matter, sets the regime.

evidence.description: Same 79 GPQA-Diamond questions: multiple-choice → β≈0; free-response (options stripped) → β=0.127 (k=10, CP [0.062,0.220]). Mean accuracy drops 0.66→0.51, best-model 0.91→0.77. 5-judge panel κ=0.73-0.92. Robust under all aggregations.

claims.statement: Naive heterogeneous fusion (mixing models of unequal quality) hurts: majority-vote gain over best member is negative on average.

evidence.description: 455 three-model triplets: mean vote gain −0.10 (hard), −0.02 (saturated). Robust under leave-one-model-out jackknife. Positive ρ-slope (+0.13 hard) not significant under model-clustering.
Consistent with Kuncheva & Whitaker [20] and Li et al. [24].

claims.statement: At matched member quality, lower error correlation buys larger diversifiable gains: heterogeneous fusion beats Self-MoA from k=3 onward.

evidence.description: Self-MoA (ρ=0.80) vs. heterogeneous (ρ=0.42), accuracy-matched 6-model band, S=9 distinct draws each. At k=3: heterogeneous wins. Across 60 resamplings: average gain +0.027 (positive in all 60). Range +0.010 to +0.050.

claims.statement: The cascade advantage over random mixing collapses to zero as the verifier AUC falls to 1/2.

evidence.description: Q(β) − Q_mix(β) = β[w(β) − (1−a_L)] + β[a_H(τ) − a_H]. With a_H(τ)=a_H (no tail adverse selection), ∫[w(β)−(1−a_L)] dβ ≥ 0 iff AUC ≥ ½, with equality iff AUC = ½.

L=GPT-5-nano (a_L=0.748), H=Opus 4.8 (a_H=0.921), verifier AUC=0.899. 20 noise-injection seeds: advantage 0.121→0.012 (seed std ≤0.005) as AUC falls 0.899→0.510. 5-fold held-out: +0.114 vs. random-mixing (cross-fold sd 0.010).

claims.statement: The volume ceiling (price-independent escalation ratio) is 1 − a_L/a_H: relative to random mixing, a calibrated cascade needs only fraction 1−a_L/a_H as many strong-model calls at fixed quality.

evidence.description: Under perfect calibration, escalated tail is L's errors until exhausted: β_cas = (q−a_L)/a_H. β_cas/β_mix → 1 − a_L/a_H. Measured: 1 − 0.748/0.921 = 0.188.

claims.statement: A deployable learned router captures essentially none of the oracle gain G on the 2026 frontier.

evidence.description: TF-IDF+domain logistic: 0.906 vs. single-best 0.901 (9% of G, CI [−0.67, 0.50]). Gradient-boosted predictor: −0.09 of G. Direct multiclass best-model predictor: −1.27. LLM-as-router (GPT-5-mini): 0% of G, routes to single-best on 100% of queries. The realizable gain is near zero not because of weak routers but because prompts carry little signal about which model is correct when the frontier disagrees.

claims.statement: The naive Pearson-of-indicators calibration overstates the co-failure gap by an order of magnitude; the correctly tetrachoric-calibrated residual is single-digit (≈2.5×).

evidence.description: MATH-500: Pearson ρ̄=0.53 → β_sf=0.0016 (spurious 32×). Tetrachoric ρ̄_tet=0.78 → β_sf=0.021 (real 2.5×). The order-of-magnitude difference is a calibration artifact, not a co-failure effect.
Century-old psychometric point [30, 32] that the LLM evaluation literature routinely elides. The paper explicitly flags that an earlier version of this analysis fell into the same trap.

claims.statement: Under a dollar budget, routing is a priced assignment with a single shadow price λ_B; every optimal policy is a per-type bang-per-buck rule.

evidence.location: Paper Appendix A.1, Proposition 4; Appendix B
evidence.description: LP dual collapses to a single scalar λ_B because per-type simplex constraints are absorbed pointwise. V(B) = min_{λ≥0} {λB + E_t max_i [qᵢ(t) − λcᵢ]}. Arg max_i [qᵢ(t) − λ_B cᵢ] bang-per-buck rule. V is nondecreasing, concave, piecewise linear; V'(B)=λ_B where differentiable.

claims.statement: The co-failure signature replicates on execution-graded competitive code, a structurally independent domain: β=0.079, full-Σ residual, tetrachoric underpricing CI excluding 1.

evidence.description: 18 models × 63 Codeforces problems (1900-3500 rating). β=0.079 (k=5, CP [0.026,0.176]). Pearson: 17× spurious. Tetrachoric: 3.1× (CI [1.5,6.2] excluding 1). Full-Σ: 1.7× residual. Co-failure is not a math artifact.

evidence_still_needed.claim-10: ≥3 seeds and held-out model selection; extension across multiple ρ levels to fit λ and predict k* out-of-sample
evidence_still_needed.claim-16: Precise code magnitude at 67-model scale with official hidden-test judge
